title: Food Preparation: Spreading Butter on Crackers
description: Children learn the practical skill of spreading butter on crackers using proper utensils and techniques. This activity develops fine motor control, hand-eye coordination, and independence in food preparation.
instructions: Set up a small tray with crackers, soft butter, a butter knife, and a plate
Demonstrate holding the knife properly with thumb and fingers
Show how to scoop a small amount of butter on the knife
Model spreading the butter gently across the cracker surface
Guide your child to try spreading butter on their own cracker
Encourage them to clean up their workspace when finished
Allow them to enjoy eating their prepared snack
Store materials properly for next use
parent_notes: This practical life activity helps children develop independence and confidence in food preparation. Follow the child's pace and allow them to repeat the activity as many times as they wish. The repetition builds muscle memory and refines their motor skills. Resist the urge to do it for them - mistakes are part of the learning process.

slides.heading: Food Preparation: Spreading Butter on Crackers
slides.subheading: Learning to prepare our own snacks
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to learn how to spread butter on crackers all by ourselves. This is such an important skill that will help you prepare your own yummy snacks!

items: Small crackers (saltines or similar)
Soft butter at room temperature
Child-safe butter knife
Small plate
Small tray
Napkins
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange all materials on the tray in an organized way, with the butter knife positioned safely.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful materials we'll use today! I always like to have everything ready before I start cooking. Can you help me make sure our butter is nice and soft?

slides.text: Hold the butter knife properly with your thumb and fingers, keeping your grip gentle but secure.
slides.mascot_explanation: Just like when I hold my honey spoon, we want to hold our knife carefully and safely. Your grown-up will show you the perfect way to hold it!

slides.text: Scoop a small amount of butter on the tip of the knife, then gently spread it across the cracker surface.
slides.mascot_explanation: We only need a little bit of butter - not too much! Then we can spread it like we're painting a beautiful picture on our cracker.

slides.prompt: Practice spreading butter on 2-3 crackers, taking your time with each one.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to try! Take your time and enjoy the process. I love watching children learn new skills - you're doing such important work!

items: Can you hold the knife safely and properly?
Are you able to scoop just the right amount of butter?
Can you spread the butter evenly across the cracker?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see how well you've learned this new skill! I'm so proud of how carefully and thoughtfully you're working. You're becoming quite the little chef!

slides.text: Clean up your workspace and enjoy eating the crackers you prepared yourself.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently spread butter on crackers with minimal assistance and proper technique.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful job you did today! Now you can make this yummy snack whenever you want. Don't forget to clean up - that's an important part of cooking too!

title: Phonogram 'th': Sound Recognition and Word Building
description: Children learn to recognize and use the 'th' phonogram in simple words. This lesson introduces both the voiced and unvoiced 'th' sounds through engaging activities and word building exercises.
instructions: Introduce the 'th' sound by having your child feel the vibration when saying 'th'
Show examples of 'th' words like 'this', 'that', 'thin', and 'thick'
Use the moveable alphabet to build simple 'th' words together
Practice reading 'th' words aloud, emphasizing the sound
Play an 'I Spy' game using objects that contain the 'th' sound
Have your child trace the letters 't' and 'h' together in sand or on paper
Create a list of 'th' words found around your home
Review all the 'th' words learned during the session
materials_needed: Sand tray or paper for tracing
parent_notes: The 'th' phonogram can be challenging as it has two different sounds (voiced as in 'this' and unvoiced as in 'think'). Don't worry about explaining this distinction - children will naturally absorb both sounds through exposure. Focus on the kinesthetic experience of feeling their tongue position and the sound vibration. Allow plenty of time for exploration and repetition.

slides.heading: Phonogram 'th': Sound Recognition and Word Building
slides.subheading: Discovering the special 'th' sound in words
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, my dear friends! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going on a sound adventure to discover the special 'th' sound. Put your tongue between your teeth and let's explore together!

items: Moveable alphabet letters
Small objects containing 'th' sounds
Sand tray or paper for tracing
Picture cards with 'th' words
Phonogram card showing 'th'
slides.setup_instructions: Organize materials on a work mat, keeping the moveable alphabet easily accessible for word building.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these exciting materials! We have special letters and pictures that will help us learn about the 'th' sound. I can't wait to build words with you!

slides.text: Place your tongue gently between your teeth and make the 'th' sound, feeling the air flow.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is such a fun sound to make! Put your tongue right between your teeth like I do. Can you feel the gentle air coming out? That's the magic of the 'th' sound!

slides.text: Use the moveable alphabet to build words like 'this', 'that', and 'thin' while saying each sound.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we get to be word builders! Let's use our special letters to make 'th' words. I love watching the letters come together to make words we can read!

slides.prompt: Practice building and reading 4-5 different 'th' words using the moveable alphabet.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is your time to be a word detective! See how many 'th' words you can build and read. Each word you make is like finding a treasure!

items: Can you make the 'th' sound correctly?
Can you identify 'th' at the beginning of words?
Are you able to build simple 'th' words with the moveable alphabet?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see how well you know the 'th' sound now! I'm so impressed with how you're learning to hear and use this special sound in words.

slides.text: Review the 'th' words you've learned and practice saying them one more time.
slides.mastery_check: Child can recognize the 'th' phonogram and use it to build simple words independently.
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful work you did today learning about the 'th' sound! Now you know another special way that letters work together. Keep listening for 'th' sounds everywhere you go!

title: Ten Board: Building Numbers 11-19 with Unit Beads
description: Children explore the structure of teen numbers using the Ten Board and golden beads. This lesson helps them understand that numbers 11-19 are composed of one ten and additional units.
instructions: Set up the Ten Board with numbers 1-10 clearly visible
Show your child a ten bar and explain it represents 'ten'
Demonstrate placing the ten bar next to different numbers (1-9) on the board
Add corresponding unit beads to show eleven, twelve, thirteen, etc.
Have your child practice building teen numbers with ten bar plus units
Count each number aloud: 'ten-one, eleven' or 'ten-two, twelve'
Practice writing the numerals 11-19 after building each with beads
Review the pattern that all teen numbers have ten plus some units
parent_notes: The Ten Board is crucial for helping children understand place value and the decimal system. This concrete experience with teen numbers prepares them for more abstract mathematical concepts later. Allow your child to physically manipulate the materials as much as needed - this hands-on exploration builds deep understanding. The key insight is that teen numbers follow a pattern: ten plus some units.

slides.heading: Ten Board: Building Numbers 11-19 with Unit Beads
slides.subheading: Discovering how teen numbers are made
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there, little mathematicians! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to discover something amazing about numbers 11 through 19. They all have a special secret - they're all made of ten plus some more!

items: Ten Board (numbers 1-10)
Golden bead ten bar
Golden bead units (1-9)
Number cards 11-19
Paper and pencil
Work mat
slides.setup_instructions: Place the Ten Board in the center of your work area with the golden beads organized nearby for easy access.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful golden beads! The long one represents ten whole units, and these smaller ones are our unit beads. Together, they'll help us build all the teen numbers!

slides.text: Place the ten bar next to number 1 on the Ten Board, then add 1 unit bead to make eleven.
slides.mascot_explanation: See how we make eleven? We take our ten bar and add just one more unit bead. It's like saying 'ten and one more' which makes eleven!

slides.text: Continue building each teen number by keeping the ten bar and changing the unit beads (2 units for twelve, 3 for thirteen, etc.).
slides.mascot_explanation: This is so exciting! Every teen number starts with our ten bar, then we just change how many unit beads we add. It's like a pattern that helps us understand numbers better!

slides.prompt: Build each number from 11-19 using the ten bar and unit beads, counting aloud as you work.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be a number builder! Take your time with each number and count out loud. I love hearing you discover how these teen numbers work!

items: Can you build any teen number using the ten bar and unit beads?
Do you understand that all teen numbers contain one ten?
Can you count from 11-19 while pointing to your bead arrangements?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see how well you understand teen numbers now! I'm so proud of how you're learning to see the patterns in our number system. You're becoming quite the mathematician!

slides.text: Review how every teen number is made of ten plus some units, and practice building a few more of your favorite teen numbers.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently build teen numbers 11-19 using the ten bar and appropriate unit beads.
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing discovery you made today! Now you know the secret of teen numbers - they all have ten inside them plus some extra units. This will help you with so many math adventures ahead!

title: Geometric Cabinet: Identifying and Matching Quadrilaterals
description: Children explore different types of quadrilaterals through hands-on manipulation and matching activities. This lesson develops visual discrimination skills and introduces geometric vocabulary for four-sided shapes.
instructions: Present the quadrilateral drawer from the Geometric Cabinet or create shapes
Remove each quadrilateral and name it: square, rectangle, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezoid
Let your child trace around each shape with their finger
Demonstrate matching each shape to its frame in the drawer
Have your child practice removing and replacing all the quadrilaterals
Go on a 'quadrilateral hunt' around your home to find similar shapes
Sort household objects by whether they are quadrilaterals or not
Create simple art by tracing around the quadrilateral shapes
materials_needed: Quadrilateral shapes (cut from cardboard if needed)
parent_notes: The Geometric Cabinet develops visual discrimination and prepares children for geometry concepts. At this age, focus more on the sensorial experience of the shapes rather than memorizing names. Let your child explore through touch and manipulation. The muscle memory of tracing shapes builds understanding that will support later mathematical learning. Encourage them to notice these shapes in their environment.

slides.heading: Geometric Cabinet: Identifying and Matching Quadrilaterals
slides.subheading: Exploring four-sided shapes all around us
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, my curious explorers! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to discover special shapes called quadrilaterals - they're shapes that have exactly four sides! Let's explore them together!

items: Quadrilateral shapes (cut from cardboard if needed)
Shape frames or outlines
Household objects with quadrilateral shapes
Paper and crayons for tracing
Work mat
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange the quadrilateral shapes and their matching frames on the work mat in an organized manner.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these wonderful shapes! Each one has four sides, but they're all different in their own special way. I love how we can touch and move them around to learn about them!

slides.text: Trace around each quadrilateral shape with your finger, feeling the four sides and four corners.
slides.mascot_explanation: Using your finger to trace around each shape is like giving it a gentle hug! Feel how each shape has exactly four sides and four corners. Isn't that interesting?

slides.text: Practice matching each quadrilateral shape to its correct frame or outline.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we get to play a matching game! Each shape has its own special home. It's like solving a puzzle when you find where each shape belongs perfectly!

slides.prompt: Explore all the quadrilateral shapes, practice matching them, and then go on a shape hunt around your home.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is such an exciting adventure! First explore all the shapes with your hands, then see if you can find shapes just like these around your house. I bet you'll be surprised at how many you can find!

items: Can you identify that each shape has four sides?
Are you able to match each quadrilateral to its frame?
Can you find quadrilateral shapes in your environment?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's see what you've learned about these four-sided shapes! I'm so impressed with how carefully you observe and explore. You're becoming quite the shape detective!

slides.text: Review the different quadrilaterals you explored and name some you found around your home.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify quadrilaterals as four-sided shapes and successfully match shapes to their frames.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful exploration of quadrilaterals! Now you know that any shape with exactly four sides is called a quadrilateral. Keep looking for them everywhere - they're all around us!

title: Read-Aloud: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes - Character Introduction
description: Children are introduced to the beloved character Chrysanthemum and explore themes of self-acceptance and the beauty of being unique. This first reading focuses on enjoying the story and connecting with the main character.
instructions: Show your child the book cover and discuss what they see
Ask them to predict what the story might be about based on the title and illustration
Read the story aloud with expression, pausing to show illustrations
Stop occasionally to ask what your child thinks about Chrysanthemum's feelings
Discuss how Chrysanthemum feels about her name at different parts of the story
Ask your child about their own name and how it makes them feel
Talk about what makes each person special and unique
End by asking what their favorite part of the story was
parent_notes: This beautiful story addresses themes of self-acceptance and dealing with teasing, which are very relevant for this age group. Allow time for your child to process the emotions in the story. Some children may relate strongly to Chrysanthemum's experience. Create a safe space for discussing feelings and emphasize the message that what makes us different makes us special. Kevin Henkes' gentle storytelling style is perfect for building emotional intelligence.

slides.heading: Read-Aloud: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes - Character Introduction
slides.subheading: Meeting a little mouse with a very special name
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, my dear story lovers! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to meet a sweet little mouse named Chrysanthemum. She has quite an interesting name, and we're going to learn about her special story together!

items: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes
Comfortable reading spot
Paper and crayons (optional for drawing)
Cozy blanket or pillow
slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading nook where you can snuggle together comfortably while enjoying the story.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make our reading time extra special by getting nice and cozy! I love when we can snuggle up together with a good book. It makes the story feel even more magical!

slides.text: Look at the book cover together and talk about what you see - notice Chrysanthemum and her expression.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this beautiful book cover! See the little mouse? That's Chrysanthemum. What do you think she might be feeling? Sometimes we can learn a lot just by looking at the pictures!

slides.text: Read the story with expression, pausing to discuss Chrysanthemum's feelings at different parts.
slides.mascot_explanation: As we read together, let's pay special attention to how Chrysanthemum feels. Sometimes she's happy, sometimes she's sad. It's okay to have different feelings - that's what makes us human, just like it makes Chrysanthemum real to us!

slides.prompt: Listen to the complete story and share your thoughts about Chrysanthemum's experiences.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's time to enjoy this wonderful story! I can't wait to hear what you think about Chrysanthemum and her adventures. Remember, there are no wrong thoughts - just your own special ideas!

items: How does Chrysanthemum feel about her name at the beginning?
What happens when she goes to school?
How does the story make you feel?
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's talk about this beautiful story! I love hearing your thoughts about the characters and what happens to them. Your ideas help make the story even more meaningful!

slides.text: Discuss what makes each person's name special and how Chrysanthemum learns to love her unique name.
slides.mastery_check: Child can retell basic story elements and express thoughts about Chrysanthemum's feelings and experiences.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful story about being special just the way you are! Just like Chrysanthemum learned to love her beautiful name, we can all celebrate what makes us unique and special. You are perfect just as you are!

title: Spreading Butter on Crackers: Controlled Hand Movements
description: Children practice the essential food preparation skill of spreading butter on crackers using controlled hand movements. This lesson develops fine motor coordination, hand strength, and independence in food preparation.
instructions: Set up a small table with all materials at child's eye level
Demonstrate how to hold the butter knife properly with fingers positioned safely
Show slow, controlled spreading motions from center outward
Allow child to practice spreading on 3-4 crackers independently
Guide child in cleaning their workspace when finished
Invite child to enjoy eating their prepared snack
Store materials properly and sanitize work area together
parent_notes: This food preparation activity builds practical independence while developing fine motor control essential for writing readiness. Allow the child to move slowly and make mistakes - this builds confidence and internal motivation. The concrete, purposeful nature of this work satisfies the child's deep need to contribute meaningfully to daily life. Resist the urge to correct or speed up the process, as the child's concentration and self-correction are more valuable than a perfect end result.

slides.heading: Spreading Butter on Crackers
slides.subheading: Controlled Hand Movements
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello there! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to help you learn how to spread butter on crackers today. This is such an important skill that will help you prepare your own delicious snacks!

items: Small plate
Soft butter in small container
Child-safe butter knife
4-5 crackers
Small napkins
Damp cloth for cleanup
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange all materials on a small table or tray at the child's eye level, with the butter knife positioned safely away from the edge.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's gather all our special materials together! I love how everything has its own place on our workspace. The butter knife is a very important tool, so we'll learn how to use it safely and carefully.

slides.text: Hold the butter knife properly with your fingers positioned safely on the handle, keeping the blade away from your body.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I hold this knife - my fingers stay on the handle part, and the flat part points away from me. This keeps us safe while we work together!

slides.text: Scoop a small amount of butter onto the knife, then place it in the center of your cracker and spread outward with gentle, controlled movements.
slides.mascot_explanation: Start with just a tiny bit of butter - we can always add more! Place it right in the middle of your cracker, then gently spread it out like you're painting a beautiful picture.

slides.prompt: Practice spreading butter on 3-4 crackers, taking your time with each one to develop smooth, controlled movements.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be the chef! Take your time with each cracker - there's no rush at all. I love watching you use such careful, gentle movements.

items: Can you show me how to hold the knife safely?
Where do we start spreading the butter on the cracker?
What do we do with our materials when we're finished?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such wonderful work! Let me ask you a few questions to see how much you've learned. I'm so proud of how carefully you're thinking about each step.

slides.text: Clean your workspace together, then enjoy eating your prepared crackers as a special snack you made yourself.
slides.mastery_check: Child can hold knife safely, spread butter with controlled movements, and clean workspace independently.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a fantastic job you did today! Now comes the best part - enjoying the delicious snack you prepared all by yourself. You should feel so proud of your careful work!

title: Geometric Cabinet Quadrilaterals: Square, Rectangle, and Rhombus Exploration
description: Children explore the quadrilateral drawer from the Geometric Cabinet, examining squares, rectangles, and rhombus shapes through visual and tactile discrimination. This lesson refines visual perception and introduces geometric vocabulary.
instructions: Present the quadrilateral drawer from the Geometric Cabinet on a work mat
Demonstrate careful removal of one shape using the knob, tracing around the empty space
Show how to trace the shape itself with two fingers before replacing it
Invite child to remove and trace each quadrilateral shape independently
Introduce vocabulary: 'This is a square,' 'This is a rectangle,' 'This is a rhombus'
Encourage child to match shapes to their corresponding spaces
Guide child in returning all shapes properly to complete the work cycle
parent_notes: This sensorial work prepares the child indirectly for geometry, mathematics, and writing through refined visual discrimination and hand coordination. The tracing movements strengthen the same muscles used in writing while developing spatial awareness. Allow the child to work at their own pace, focusing on the process rather than speed or perfection. The repetitive nature of removing, tracing, and replacing shapes builds concentration and attention to detail that will serve them throughout their academic journey.

slides.heading: Geometric Cabinet Quadrilaterals
slides.subheading: Square, Rectangle, and Rhombus Exploration
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm excited to explore some very special shapes with you today. These shapes have four sides and they each have their own special name and characteristics!

items: Geometric Cabinet quadrilateral drawer
Work mat or small rug
Geometric Cabinet cards (optional)
Small basket for removed shapes
slides.setup_instructions: Place the work mat on the table, then carefully carry the quadrilateral drawer and set it in the center of the mat.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this beautiful wooden drawer filled with amazing shapes! Each shape has its own special place, and we'll learn how to handle them very carefully with our gentle hands.

slides.text: Carefully remove one shape by holding its knob, then trace around the empty space with two fingers.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I use just my pointer and middle finger to trace around the empty space. This helps our fingers remember the shape even when we can't see it!

slides.text: Trace around the shape itself with the same two fingers, feeling its edges and corners before replacing it in the correct space.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now we trace the shape itself - feel how smooth the edges are and count those corners! This helps our hands learn what makes each shape special and different from the others.

slides.prompt: Remove, trace, and replace each quadrilateral shape in the drawer, taking time to feel the differences between squares, rectangles, and rhombus shapes.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to explore each shape! Take your time feeling the differences - some have equal sides, some have longer and shorter sides. Your fingers are excellent teachers!

items: Which shape has four equal sides and four equal corners?
Can you show me how to trace a shape properly?
What makes the rectangle different from the square?
slides.mascot_explanation: You've done such careful work exploring these shapes! Let's see what your fingers and eyes have discovered about these wonderful four-sided shapes.

slides.text: Return all shapes to their proper places in the drawer, ensuring each one fits perfectly in its designated space.
slides.mastery_check: Child can remove shapes using knob, trace both frame and inset accurately, and return shapes to correct positions independently.
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful exploring you did today! You used your fingers to learn about squares, rectangles, and rhombus shapes. Each shape found its way back to its perfect home in the drawer!

title: South America Puzzle Map: Country Shapes and Placement
description: Children work with the South America puzzle map, learning to identify and place individual country pieces while developing spatial awareness and geographic knowledge. This lesson introduces South American countries through hands-on manipulation.
instructions: Present the South America puzzle map frame with all pieces removed and placed nearby
Demonstrate careful handling of one country piece, showing how to lift by the knob
Show how to identify the correct placement by matching the shape to the empty space
Model placing the piece gently, ensuring proper fit without forcing
Invite child to select and place country pieces one at a time
Encourage child to name colors and observe different sizes of countries
Guide completion of the entire puzzle, celebrating each successful placement
parent_notes: This geography work builds spatial intelligence, fine motor control, and global awareness simultaneously. The puzzle format makes abstract geography concepts concrete and manageable for young minds. Encourage the child to work slowly and observe the different shapes and sizes of countries, laying groundwork for later cultural studies. The self-correcting nature of the puzzle builds independence and problem-solving skills. Don't worry about memorizing country names at this stage - focus on spatial relationships and careful handling of materials.

slides.heading: South America Puzzle Map
slides.subheading: Country Shapes and Placement
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello there! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to explore the continent of South America with you today. We'll discover all the different countries and their unique shapes together!

items: South America puzzle map
Work mat or table surface
Small tray for removed pieces
Control chart (optional)
slides.setup_instructions: Place the work mat on the table, set the puzzle frame in the center, and arrange the country pieces on the tray beside the frame.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this beautiful puzzle of South America! Each colored piece represents a different country, and they all fit together like one big, wonderful puzzle. Isn't that amazing?

slides.text: Pick up one country piece by holding its knob carefully, then examine its shape and color.
slides.mascot_explanation: Always use the little knob to pick up each piece - this keeps the puzzle safe and helps your fingers get stronger! Look at the interesting shape of each country piece.

slides.text: Look at the empty spaces in the frame and find where your piece belongs by matching its shape to the corresponding space.
slides.mascot_explanation: Each country has its own special shape and its own special place! Use your eyes to look carefully at the empty spaces - which one matches your piece perfectly?

slides.prompt: Complete the South America puzzle by placing each country piece in its correct location, working slowly and observing the different shapes and sizes.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be the geography detective! Take your time finding where each country belongs. Some are big, some are small, and each one has its own special shape.

items: Can you show me how to pick up a puzzle piece properly?
Which country piece is the largest/smallest?
How do you know where a piece belongs in the puzzle?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such careful work with our South America puzzle! I love watching you use your eyes and hands to figure out where each country belongs.

slides.text: Admire your completed South America puzzle, then carefully remove pieces one by one and return them to the tray for the next person.
slides.mastery_check: Child can handle puzzle pieces by the knob, match shapes to spaces accurately, and complete puzzle independently.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful job you did completing South America! Now we'll carefully take it apart so the next friend can enjoy this wonderful geography work too. You're becoming quite the world explorer!

title: Animal Classification Introduction: Vertebrate and Invertebrate Sorting Cards
description: Children begin exploring animal classification by sorting picture cards into two basic categories: animals with backbones (vertebrates) and animals without backbones (invertebrates). This lesson introduces fundamental biological classification concepts through concrete materials.
instructions: Prepare two sorting mats labeled 'Animals with Backbones' and 'Animals without Backbones'
Present a collection of animal picture cards showing various vertebrates and invertebrates
Demonstrate by selecting one card and discussing whether the animal has a backbone
Show placement of the first card on the appropriate mat with clear explanation
Invite child to select cards and determine the correct category for each animal
Guide discussions about what makes an animal have or not have a backbone
Review sorted cards together, reinforcing the concepts learned
materials_needed: Animal picture cards (mix of vertebrates and invertebrates)
parent_notes: This introduction to scientific classification builds logical thinking and observational skills while satisfying the child's natural curiosity about the animal world. At this age, focus on the concrete concept of having or not having a backbone rather than complex scientific terminology. Encourage the child to think about their own backbone and how it feels, making the concept personal and tangible. This work prepares for more detailed classification studies and develops the scientific mind through hands-on categorization activities.

slides.heading: Animal Classification Introduction
slides.subheading: Vertebrate and Invertebrate Sorting Cards
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to explore the animal kingdom with you today. We're going to learn something very special about animals - whether they have backbones like you and me!

items: Animal picture cards (mix of vertebrates and invertebrates)
Two sorting mats or papers
Labels reading 'With Backbones' and 'Without Backbones'
Small basket for cards
slides.setup_instructions: Place the two sorting mats on the work surface, attach the labels, and set the basket of animal cards between them for easy access.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful animal pictures! We have two special places to sort them - one for animals with backbones and one for animals without backbones. Let me show you how this works!

slides.text: Feel your own backbone by running your hand down your back, then explain that some animals have backbones like us and some don't.
slides.mascot_explanation: Can you feel your backbone? It's those bumpy bones that go down your back! As a bear, I have a backbone too. Some animals have them just like us, and some animals don't have them at all.

slides.text: Select an animal card and discuss together whether that animal has a backbone, then place it on the appropriate sorting mat.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's pick a card together! Look at this animal carefully - does it have a backbone inside its body like we do, or is it the kind of animal that doesn't have one? Let's think about it together!

slides.prompt: Sort the remaining animal cards into 'with backbones' and 'without backbones' categories, discussing each choice as you work.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you're the animal scientist! Pick each card and think carefully about whether that animal has a backbone or not. Take your time and trust what you know about each animal.

items: Can you name an animal that has a backbone?
Can you name an animal that doesn't have a backbone?
Where is your backbone located in your body?
slides.mascot_explanation: You've done such wonderful thinking about animals today! Let's see how much you've learned about which animals have backbones and which ones don't. You're becoming quite the animal expert!

slides.text: Review all sorted cards together, celebrating correct classifications and gently correcting any misunderstandings through discussion.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify the concept of backbone, sort most animals correctly, and explain their reasoning for classification choices.
slides.mascot_explanation: What amazing work you did learning about animal backbones today! You discovered that some animals are like us with backbones, and some are very different without them. The animal world is so fascinating!

title: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes: Names Make Us Special Story Circle
description: Children listen to and discuss 'Chrysanthemum' by Kevin Henkes, exploring themes of self-acceptance, uniqueness of names, and handling teasing. This story promotes emotional development and celebrates individual differences.
instructions: Create a cozy reading circle with cushions or carpet area for comfortable listening
Introduce the book by showing the cover and asking children to predict the story
Read 'Chrysanthemum' aloud with expression, pausing for reactions and questions
Discuss how Chrysanthemum felt about her name and how others treated her
Invite children to share what makes their own names special or unique
Explore strategies for handling situations when others aren't kind about differences
Connect the story to real-life experiences about feeling different or special
materials_needed: Paper and crayons for name writing activity
parent_notes: This read-aloud supports social-emotional development by addressing the sensitive topic of teasing and self-acceptance. Children this age are developing their sense of identity and may relate to Chrysanthemum's experiences. Encourage authentic discussion about feelings and validate children's responses to the story. Use this opportunity to reinforce positive messages about uniqueness and the importance of treating others with kindness. The story also naturally introduces rich vocabulary and provides opportunities for personal connections through name discussions.

slides.heading: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes
slides.subheading: Names Make Us Special Story Circle
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello friends! I'm Betsy Bear and I have a very special story to share with you today. It's about a little mouse girl with a very beautiful but unusual name - just like how each of your names is special too!

items: 'Chrysanthemum' by Kevin Henkes
Comfortable seating cushions or rug
Paper and crayons for name writing activity
Small mirror (optional)
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange cushions or rug in a circle where everyone can see the book clearly, with art materials nearby for the follow-up activity.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's make our cozy story circle together! I love how we all sit together to share stories - it makes the story even more special when we experience it together as friends.

slides.text: Look at the book cover together and ask children to predict what they think the story might be about based on the title and illustration.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this beautiful cover! The little mouse looks so sweet, and what an interesting name she has. What do you think might happen in a story about someone named Chrysanthemum?

slides.text: Read the story aloud with expression, pausing at key moments to observe children's reactions and allow for natural comments or questions.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's dive into Chrysanthemum's story together! I'll read with lots of feeling, and you can share your thoughts whenever something important happens in the story.

slides.prompt: Discuss the story and share what makes each child's name special, then draw or write their names on paper with decorative elements.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful story about names and being special! Now let's talk about your own beautiful names and what makes each one unique and important, just like Chrysanthemum's name.

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel when others teased her about her name?
What changed Chrysanthemum's feelings about her name?
What makes your name special to you?
slides.mascot_explanation: You listened so carefully to Chrysanthemum's story! Let's talk about the important things that happened and how the characters felt. Stories help us understand our own feelings too.

slides.text: Conclude by reinforcing that everyone's name is special and unique, just like each person, and that differences should be celebrated rather than teased.
slides.mastery_check: Child can retell key story events, identify character emotions, and make personal connections to the theme of celebrating uniqueness.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a beautiful story about loving yourself just the way you are! Remember, your name is perfect for you, just like Chrysanthemum's name was perfect for her. We're all special and unique in our own wonderful ways!

title: Spreading Butter: Breakfast Preparation Skills
description: Children learn the careful motions of spreading butter on toast using proper knife handling techniques. This activity develops fine motor control and practical self-care skills for meal preparation.
instructions: Set up a small tray with butter, toast, child-safe knife, and plate at child's workspace
Demonstrate how to hold the knife properly with fingers away from the blade
Show slow, gentle spreading motions from center outward
Let child practice spreading butter on their own piece of toast
Guide child to clean up materials and wash the knife
Encourage child to enjoy eating their prepared toast
Discuss how this skill helps them prepare their own breakfast
Store materials for future breakfast preparation practice
parent_notes: This activity builds independence in food preparation while developing precise hand movements. The child experiences the satisfaction of preparing their own food, which builds confidence and practical life skills. Allow the child to work at their own pace and resist the urge to help unless they ask. The focus is on the process and developing coordination, not perfect results.

slides.heading: Spreading Butter
slides.subheading: Learning breakfast preparation skills
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear, and I love making my own breakfast! Today we're going to learn how to spread butter on toast just like grown-ups do. It's going to be so tasty!

items: Small spreading knife (child-safe)
Softened butter in small container
2 pieces of toast
Small plates
Kitchen towel
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange all materials on a small tray at the child's workspace. Make sure butter is soft enough to spread easily.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these special breakfast tools! We have a safe knife just your size and some yummy butter. Let's set everything up on our tray so we can make the most delicious toast together!

slides.text: Show child how to hold the knife safely with fingers away from the blade, gripping the handle firmly.
slides.mascot_explanation: Safety first! Hold your knife like this - fingers on the handle, not near the sharp part. I always hold my honey spoon the same careful way when I'm making my breakfast!

slides.text: Demonstrate slow, gentle spreading motions from the center of the toast outward to the edges.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I spread from the middle and work my way out to the edges! It's like painting a picture, but with butter. Slow and gentle movements make the best toast!

slides.prompt: Let your child practice spreading butter on their own piece of toast, offering gentle guidance only when needed.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn! Take your time and spread that butter however feels right to you. I know you can do it, and I can't wait to see how wonderful your toast turns out!

items: Can the child hold the knife safely?
Do they use gentle spreading motions?
Can they spread butter across most of the toast?
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such a great job! I can see you're holding your knife safely and making smooth spreading motions. Your toast looks absolutely delicious and ready to eat!

slides.text: Help child clean up materials, wash the knife, and enjoy eating their prepared toast together.
slides.mastery_check: Child can hold knife safely and spread butter with controlled motions across the toast surface.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful breakfast you made all by yourself! Don't forget to clean up - that's part of being a great cook. Now let's enjoy this yummy toast you prepared!

title: Building CVC Words: Short O Sound Practice
description: Children use the Moveable Alphabet to build consonant-vowel-consonant words focusing on short O sounds like mop, dog, and pot. This reinforces phonetic awareness and early spelling skills through hands-on letter manipulation.
instructions: Set up Moveable Alphabet box with focus on letters needed for short O words
Review the short O sound by having child identify it in spoken words
Start with the word 'dog' - have child find and arrange the letters d-o-g
Continue with 'mop', 'pot', 'hot', and 'box' as child builds confidence
Encourage child to sound out each letter as they place it
Let child try building their own short O words if they're ready
Practice reading the words they built by pointing to each letter
Store alphabet materials neatly for future word building sessions
materials_needed: List of short O words for reference
Small objects (toy dog, mop, pot) optional
parent_notes: The Moveable Alphabet allows children to build words before they can write them perfectly, separating the mental work of spelling from the physical challenge of writing. Focus on the sounds rather than perfect letter formation. If the child struggles, go back to oral phonetic games and return to this activity later. The goal is to make the connection between sounds and symbols concrete and enjoyable.

slides.heading: Building CVC Words
slides.subheading: Short O sound practice with moveable alphabet
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello! I'm Betsy Bear and I love spelling words with letters! Today we're going to build words that have the 'o' sound, like in 'dog' and 'mop'. It's like building with letter blocks!

items: Moveable Alphabet (wooden or plastic letters)
Small mat for letter work
List of short O words for reference
Small objects (toy dog, mop, pot) optional
slides.setup_instructions: Place the Moveable Alphabet box and mat in child's workspace. Have reference list nearby but out of direct view.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful letters waiting to become words! We'll use our special mat to keep everything organized, just like I organize my honey pots in my bear cave!

slides.text: Review the short O sound with your child by saying it clearly and having them repeat it back.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's practice our 'o' sound together - it sounds like 'ahhh' when you're surprised! Can you make that sound with me? It's the sound in the middle of so many fun words!

slides.text: Start with 'dog' - help child find the letters d, o, and g from the alphabet and arrange them in order.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's build the word 'dog'! First we need a 'd', then our special 'o', and finally a 'g'. It's like putting together a puzzle that makes a word!

slides.prompt: Let child build more short O words: mop, pot, hot, box. Encourage them to sound out each letter as they work.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be the word builder! Try making 'mop' or 'pot' or any other short O word you can think of. I love watching you figure out which letters make which sounds!

items: Can the child identify the short O sound?
Do they place letters in the correct sequence?
Can they sound out the completed words?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a good word builder! I can hear you making the right sounds for each letter, and you're putting them in just the right order to make real words!

slides.text: Have child read back the words they built, then help organize the letters back into the alphabet box.
slides.mastery_check: Child can build at least 3 short O CVC words using the moveable alphabet and sound them out when reading.
slides.mascot_explanation: What amazing words you built today! Let's read them one more time together, and then we'll put our letters away so they're ready for our next word-building adventure!

title: Ten Board: Finding Number Partners to 10
description: Children explore number combinations that make 10 using the Ten Board with red and blue counters. This concrete activity builds understanding of number bonds and prepares for addition and subtraction work.
instructions: Set up Ten Board (1x10 grid) with red and blue counters in separate containers
Show child how 10 red counters fill the entire board completely
Replace one red counter with blue, discussing how it's still 10 total (9+1)
Continue systematically: 8 red + 2 blue, 7 red + 3 blue, etc.
Let child discover and create their own combinations that make 10
Have child verbalize each combination: 'Seven and three make ten'
Practice a few combinations in random order to reinforce learning
Store materials neatly and discuss what patterns child noticed
materials_needed: Ten Board (wooden board with 10 squares in a row)
parent_notes: The Ten Board makes abstract number relationships concrete and visual. This prepares children for mental math by showing them all the ways to make 10, which is fundamental for base-10 understanding. Let the child discover the patterns rather than telling them directly. Some children may need several sessions to internalize these combinations, and that's perfectly normal. The physical manipulation helps build neural pathways for mathematical thinking.

slides.heading: Ten Board
slides.subheading: Finding number partners that make 10
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi! I'm Betsy Bear, and I love playing with numbers! Today we're going to discover all the different ways to make the number 10 using our special Ten Board and colorful counters!

items: Ten Board (wooden board with 10 squares in a row)
Red counters (at least 10)
Blue counters (at least 10)
Small containers for counters
slides.setup_instructions: Place Ten Board horizontally in front of child. Put red and blue counters in separate small containers within easy reach.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at our special Ten Board with all these squares waiting to be filled! And see these pretty red and blue counters? We're going to use them to make the number 10 in lots of different ways!

slides.text: Demonstrate filling all 10 squares with red counters, counting aloud as you place each one.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch as I fill up every single square with red counters! One, two, three... all the way to ten! When all the squares are full, we have exactly 10 counters on our board!

slides.text: Replace one red counter with a blue one, showing child that we still have 10 total counters (9 red + 1 blue = 10).
slides.mascot_explanation: Now I'm going to switch one red counter for a blue one! Look - we still have all 10 squares filled, but now it's 9 red and 1 blue. That still makes 10 altogether!

slides.prompt: Let child explore creating different combinations of red and blue counters that fill all 10 squares, encouraging them to verbalize each combination.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be a number detective! Try making different patterns with red and blue counters, but always keep all 10 squares filled. What combinations can you discover?

items: Can the child create multiple combinations that equal 10?
Do they understand that all squares must be filled?
Can they verbalize the combinations (like '6 and 4 make 10')?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're doing such great math work! I can see you understand that no matter how we arrange the red and blue counters, we always need exactly 10 to fill our board!

slides.text: Review 2-3 of the combinations child discovered, then help organize counters back into containers.
slides.mastery_check: Child can create at least 4 different combinations of red and blue counters that total 10 and verbalize the number sentences.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful number explorer you are! You found so many ways to make 10 with red and blue counters. Let's put everything away so it's ready for more math adventures!

title: Geometric Cabinet: Exploring Quadrilaterals
description: Children work with square, rectangle, rhombus, and trapezoid from the Geometric Cabinet, feeling their edges and exploring their properties. This sensorial experience builds geometric vocabulary and visual discrimination skills.
instructions: Select 4 quadrilateral shapes from Geometric Cabinet: square, rectangle, rhombus, trapezoid
Present shapes one at a time, tracing edges with two fingers while naming each
Have child trace each shape's outline using the same two-finger technique
Discuss what makes each shape special (4 sides, different angles, etc.)
Let child feel for similarities and differences between the shapes
Practice matching shapes to their corresponding frames in the cabinet
Encourage child to find objects in the room that match these shapes
Return shapes to cabinet, maintaining organization and care of materials
materials_needed: Square, rectangle, rhombus, and trapezoid shapes
parent_notes: The Geometric Cabinet isolates geometric concepts through tactile exploration before introducing abstract names. The two-finger tracing technique helps children internalize the shape through muscle memory. Don't rush to teach all the shape names at once - focus on the sensorial experience first. Some children may need multiple exposures before distinguishing between similar shapes like squares and rhombuses. The goal is building geometric sense, not memorization.

slides.heading: Geometric Cabinet
slides.subheading: Exploring quadrilaterals with four sides
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello there! I'm Betsy Bear, and I love exploring shapes! Today we're going to feel and learn about special shapes that all have four sides - they're called quadrilaterals. What a fun word!

items: Geometric Cabinet (quadrilateral drawer)
Small work mat
Square, rectangle, rhombus, and trapezoid shapes
Corresponding frames from cabinet
slides.setup_instructions: Place work mat in front of child. Carefully remove the 4 quadrilateral shapes and their frames from the cabinet drawer.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful wooden shapes from our special Geometric Cabinet! Each one has four sides but they're all different. Let's put them on our mat so we can explore them together!

slides.text: Present the square first, tracing its edges slowly with your index and middle fingers while naming it clearly.
slides.mascot_explanation: This is a square! Watch how I trace around its edges with two fingers - this helps me feel its shape. See how all four sides are the same length? That's what makes it special!

slides.text: Have child trace each quadrilateral shape using the same two-finger technique, feeling the edges and corners.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn! Use two fingers just like I did and trace around each shape. Feel how the edges and corners are different for each one. What do you notice about them?

slides.prompt: Let child explore matching each shape to its corresponding frame and practice distinguishing between the four quadrilaterals through touch and sight.
slides.mascot_explanation: Time to be a shape detective! Try matching each shape to its special frame, and see how each four-sided shape is unique. I love how carefully you're exploring each one!

items: Can the child trace the shapes with proper finger technique?
Do they notice that all shapes have four sides?
Can they match shapes to their corresponding frames?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're such a good shape explorer! I can see you're tracing carefully and noticing how each shape has four sides but feels different. That's exactly what real mathematicians do!

slides.text: Help child carefully return all shapes and frames to their proper places in the Geometric Cabinet drawer.
slides.mastery_check: Child can trace quadrilateral shapes with two fingers and successfully match each shape to its corresponding frame.
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful shape work you did today! Now let's put each shape back in its special home in the cabinet so they're ready for our next geometric adventure together!

title: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes: Celebrating Name Uniqueness
description: Children listen to the story of Chrysanthemum, a young mouse who learns to love her unique name despite teasing from classmates. This story promotes self-acceptance and celebrates individual differences.
instructions: Gather child comfortably for story time, showing the book cover and title
Discuss the main character's very long name and how it might feel different
Read the story with expression, pausing to discuss Chrysanthemum's feelings
Ask child how they would feel if someone made fun of their name
Talk about what makes each person's name special and meaningful
Encourage child to share something they like about their own name
Discuss how the teacher Mrs. Chud helped Chrysanthemum feel better
Connect the story to treating others with kindness and celebrating differences
materials_needed: Comfortable reading area with pillows or chairs
Paper and crayons for optional name art activity
parent_notes: This story addresses the important themes of self-acceptance and treating others with kindness. Some children may relate to feeling different or being teased. Be prepared to have gentle conversations about these experiences. The story shows how an understanding adult can help children feel valued. Use this as an opportunity to celebrate your child's unique qualities and discuss how differences make the world more interesting.

slides.heading: Chrysanthemum
slides.subheading: A story about loving your unique name by Kevin Henkes
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi! I'm Betsy Bear, and I think every name is special, just like every bear is special! Today we're reading about a little mouse named Chrysanthemum who has a very long, beautiful name like a flower!

items: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes
Comfortable reading area with pillows or chairs
Paper and crayons for optional name art activity
slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading space where you and your child can sit comfortably together to enjoy the story.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get cozy for our story time! I love snuggling up with a good book, and this one is about a mouse who learns something very important about herself and her name!

slides.text: Show the book cover and read the title, discussing how Chrysanthemum is both a name and a type of flower.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this beautiful cover! Chrysanthemum is a very special name - it's the name of a pretty flower too. I wonder how it feels to have such a long, flowery name!

slides.text: Read the story with expression, pausing when Chrysanthemum feels sad to discuss her emotions with your child.
slides.mascot_explanation: As we read, let's pay attention to how Chrysanthemum feels. Sometimes she's happy, and sometimes she feels sad. It's okay to have different feelings, and we can talk about them together!

slides.prompt: After reading, discuss what makes each person's name special and encourage child to share something they like about their own name.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now let's talk about names! Every name has a story and is special in its own way. I'd love to hear what you like about your name, and I'll tell you what I like about mine too!

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel when others teased her?
What helped her feel better about her name?
How can we be kind to people who seem different?
slides.mascot_explanation: You understood this story so well! You can see how Chrysanthemum's feelings changed and how important it is to be kind to everyone, especially when they might feel different or sad!

slides.text: Connect the story to real life by discussing how we can celebrate what makes each person unique and special.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify Chrysanthemum's feelings throughout the story and explain how kindness helped her feel better about her name.
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful story about being proud of who you are! Just like Chrysanthemum learned to love her special name, we can all celebrate the things that make us unique and special!

title: Food Preparation: Spreading Butter on Toast Triangles
description: Children learn the precise movements of spreading butter evenly on toast using a butter knife. This practical life activity develops fine motor control and hand-eye coordination while preparing a nutritious snack.
instructions: 1. Prepare toast slices and set up spreading station with butter, knife, and cutting board
2. Show child how to hold the butter knife properly with thumb and fingers
3. Demonstrate scooping a small amount of butter with the knife edge
4. Model spreading motion from center outward in smooth strokes
5. Guide child to cut toast diagonally into triangles after spreading
6. Have child clean knife and workspace when finished
7. Enjoy the prepared snack together and discuss the process
8. Store materials properly for next use
parent_notes: This activity builds independence and practical skills while satisfying the child's desire to contribute meaningfully to daily life. Allow plenty of time for the child to work at their own pace, resisting the urge to take over when movements seem slow or imperfect. The focus is on the process and developing coordination rather than achieving a perfect result. Encourage concentration by minimizing distractions and offering gentle guidance only when needed.

slides.heading: Food Preparation: Spreading Butter on Toast Triangles
slides.subheading: Learning to prepare our own snack with careful movements
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there! I'm Betsy Bear and I love making my own snacks. Today we're going to learn how to spread butter on toast just like grown-ups do, and then cut it into pretty triangles!

items: 2-3 slices of toast
Softened butter in small bowl
Child-sized butter knife
Small cutting board
Cloth for cleanup
slides.setup_instructions: Place all materials on a low table or counter where your child can work comfortably. Make sure the butter is soft enough to spread easily.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these special tools we get to use! The butter knife is just the right size for your hands, and the cutting board will keep our workspace nice and clean.

slides.text: Show your child how to hold the butter knife properly, with thumb on one side and fingers on the other side of the handle.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I hold my knife - it's like giving it a gentle hug with my fingers! This helps us control the knife and keeps our hands safe while we work.

slides.text: Demonstrate scooping a small amount of butter with the edge of the knife, then spreading it on toast using smooth, even strokes from center outward.
slides.mascot_explanation: See how I take just a little bit of butter first? Then I spread it gently like I'm painting a picture on the toast, making sure every spot gets covered evenly.

slides.prompt: Let your child practice spreading butter on their own piece of toast, offering gentle guidance as needed. Then help them cut the toast into triangular pieces.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be the chef! Take your time and spread that butter however feels right to you - there's no rush when we're learning something new.

items: Can you show me how to hold the butter knife safely?
What direction do we spread the butter on the toast?
How do we make triangle shapes from our square toast?
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such a wonderful job! Let's talk about what we learned so you can remember these special skills for next time you want to make a snack.

slides.text: Clean up materials together and enjoy eating the prepared snack. Discuss how it feels to prepare your own food.
slides.mastery_check: Child can hold knife properly and spread butter with basic coordination, attempting to cut toast into triangles
slides.mascot_explanation: What a delicious snack you made all by yourself! I'm so proud of how carefully you worked and how you cleaned up everything when you were done.

title: Geometric Cabinet: Exploring Quadrilaterals with Touch and Sight
description: Children explore different quadrilateral shapes through tactile and visual discrimination using geometric cabinet pieces. This sensorial work refines perception of angles, sides, and geometric relationships.
instructions: 1. Present the quadrilateral drawer from geometric cabinet or create shapes from cardboard
2. Remove one shape at a time, tracing the outline with your finger
3. Show child how to trace each shape clockwise starting from top left
4. Name each quadrilateral: square, rectangle, rhombus, trapezoid
5. Have child close eyes and identify shapes through touch alone
6. Practice matching shapes to their corresponding frames
7. Discuss how shapes are the same and different
8. Return pieces to proper places in organized manner
materials_needed: Geometric cabinet quadrilateral drawer or cardboard cutouts
parent_notes: The geometric cabinet refines visual and tactile discrimination while building vocabulary for geometric concepts. Emphasize the tracing movement as this prepares the hand for writing while imprinting the shape in the child's muscle memory. Allow the child to explore at their own pace and resist correcting immediately - let them discover differences through their own observation. The sensorial materials are designed to be self-correcting, so the child will naturally notice when pieces don't fit properly.

slides.heading: Geometric Cabinet: Exploring Quadrilaterals with Touch and Sight
slides.subheading: Discovering four-sided shapes through our senses
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, my curious friend! I'm Betsy Bear and I love exploring shapes with my paws. Today we're going to touch and see some very special four-sided shapes called quadrilaterals!

items: Geometric cabinet quadrilateral drawer or cardboard cutouts
Wooden tray for organizing shapes
Small cloth for cleaning pieces
Blindfold or scarf (optional)
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange materials on a mat or table where your child can move freely around the shapes. Ensure good lighting for visual work.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful wooden shapes! Each one has four sides, but they're all different in their own special way. We'll use our eyes and our fingers to learn about them.

slides.text: Show your child how to trace each quadrilateral shape clockwise starting from the top left, using the index and middle finger together.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch how I trace around this shape with two fingers together! We always start at the top left and go around like the hands of a clock - this helps our fingers remember the shape.

slides.text: Name each shape as you trace it: square, rectangle, rhombus, and trapezoid. Emphasize the four sides that each shape has in common.
slides.mascot_explanation: Each shape has a special name, just like you do! Square, rectangle, rhombus, trapezoid - they all have four sides but look at how different they are!

slides.prompt: Have your child practice tracing the shapes with eyes open, then try identifying them by touch alone. Take turns matching shapes to their frames.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be a shape detective! Can you figure out which shape is which just by touching it? Your fingers are very smart and can learn to recognize each shape.

items: How many sides does each of these shapes have?
Can you trace the square and tell me about its corners?
Which shape can you identify just by touching it?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're becoming such a shape expert! Let's talk about what makes these four-sided shapes special and how your fingers can tell them apart.

slides.text: Return all pieces to their proper places in the geometric cabinet. Discuss which shapes were easiest and hardest to identify by touch.
slides.mastery_check: Child can trace shapes with proper finger technique and identify at least 2-3 quadrilaterals by touch
slides.mascot_explanation: What a wonderful job exploring these quadrilaterals! Your fingers have learned so much today, and now you can find these shapes everywhere you look in the world around us.

title: Animal Classification: Vertebrate and Invertebrate Sorting with Picture Cards
description: Children learn to distinguish between animals that have backbones (vertebrates) and those that don't (invertebrates) through hands-on sorting activities. This introduces basic biological classification concepts through concrete examples.
instructions: 1. Gather picture cards or small figures of various animals (fish, birds, mammals, insects, spiders, worms)
2. Explain that all animals can be put into two big groups based on their backbones
3. Show examples of vertebrates (animals with backbones) using pictures and pointing to spine
4. Show examples of invertebrates (animals without backbones) and explain the difference
5. Have child sort remaining animal cards into two labeled groups
6. Discuss interesting facts about each group and their characteristics
7. Encourage child to feel their own backbone and understand they are vertebrates
8. Clean up materials and review the two main animal groups learned
parent_notes: This lesson introduces abstract classification concepts through concrete materials and experiences. At this age, focus on the simple distinction of backbone versus no backbone rather than diving into complex taxonomic details. Encourage the child to make connections to their own body and animals they know personally. The goal is to plant seeds of scientific thinking and observation skills rather than memorization of facts. Allow for questions and wonder, and it's perfectly fine to say 'I don't know, let's find out together' when faced with challenging questions.

slides.heading: Animal Classification: Vertebrate and Invertebrate Sorting with Picture Cards
slides.subheading: Discovering animals with and without backbones
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there, little scientist! I'm Betsy Bear and I love learning about all the amazing animals in our world. Today we're going to sort animals into two special groups - it's like organizing toys, but with real animals!

items: Picture cards of 10-12 different animals
Two sorting trays or mats
Labels reading 'Vertebrates' and 'Invertebrates'
Magnifying glass for detailed observation
Simple anatomy book or poster (optional)
slides.setup_instructions: Arrange the two sorting areas with labels clearly visible. Have animal cards mixed up and ready for sorting.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at all these wonderful animal pictures we get to explore! We have special places to sort them and even a magnifying glass to see tiny details - just like real scientists use!

slides.text: Explain that all animals fit into two big groups: those with backbones (vertebrates) and those without backbones (invertebrates). Show examples of each.
slides.mascot_explanation: Did you know that every single animal in the whole world fits into one of two groups? Some animals have a backbone like you and me, and some don't - isn't that amazing?

slides.text: Have your child feel their own backbone by running their hand down their spine, then explain that some animals have this and others don't.
slides.mascot_explanation: Feel that bumpy line going down your back? That's your backbone, and it's what makes you a vertebrate just like dogs, cats, birds, and fish! Pretty cool, right?

slides.prompt: Let your child sort the animal picture cards into vertebrate and invertebrate groups, discussing each animal as they place it and why it belongs in that category.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be an animal scientist! Look at each picture carefully and decide - does this animal have a backbone like you, or is it different? Take your time and think about each one.

items: Can you tell me what makes an animal a vertebrate?
Which group do insects like butterflies belong to?
What kind of animal are you - vertebrate or invertebrate?
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such fantastic sorting work! Let's make sure we understand these two important animal groups so you can think about them when you see animals outside.

slides.text: Review the sorted groups together and discuss how this classification helps scientists study animals. Encourage looking for vertebrates and invertebrates during outdoor time.
slides.mastery_check: Child can identify the basic difference between vertebrates and invertebrates and sort at least 6-8 animals correctly
slides.mascot_explanation: What an amazing animal scientist you are! Now when you go outside, you can look at any animal and figure out which group it belongs to. Science is everywhere around us!

title: South America Puzzle Map: Exploring Countries and Landforms Together
description: Children work with the South America puzzle map to identify countries, major geographical features, and begin understanding the continent's diversity. This geography lesson builds spatial awareness and cultural appreciation.
instructions: 1. Present the South America puzzle map on a mat or table
2. Remove all pieces and mix them gently, naming countries as you go
3. Show child how to hold puzzle pieces by the knobs to develop pincer grip
4. Begin replacing pieces together, starting with largest countries like Brazil
5. Point out major geographical features like the Amazon River and Andes Mountains
6. Discuss different colors representing different countries on the map
7. Have child practice removing and replacing pieces independently
8. Store puzzle pieces carefully and discuss what was learned about South America
materials_needed: South America puzzle map (or printed/cardboard version)
Globe or world map for reference
parent_notes: The puzzle maps are designed to give children a concrete, hands-on experience with geography before abstract map reading. The knobs on each piece develop the pincer grip needed for writing while the colors and shapes create visual and tactile memories of each country. Focus on the sensorial experience rather than memorizing country names at this stage - the goal is to build familiarity and interest in the world's diversity. Use the three-period lesson technique to introduce country names gradually over multiple sessions.

slides.heading: South America Puzzle Map: Exploring Countries and Landforms Together
slides.subheading: Discovering the continent of South America through hands-on mapping
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello, my young explorer! I'm Betsy Bear and I love discovering new places around our beautiful world. Today we're going to explore South America with a special puzzle map that we can touch and build together!

items: South America puzzle map (or printed/cardboard version)
Control chart showing completed map
Small mat for workspace
Globe or world map for reference
slides.setup_instructions: Set up the puzzle map on a clean, flat surface with the control chart nearby for reference. Have the globe ready to show South America's position in the world.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at these beautiful puzzle pieces! Each one is a different country in South America, and they all fit together like one big family. We can touch and feel each piece to learn about this amazing continent!

slides.text: Show your child how to hold each puzzle piece by its knob using thumb and forefinger, demonstrating the pincer grip while removing pieces from the map.
slides.mascot_explanation: See how I hold each piece with just my thumb and pointer finger on the little knob? This helps our fingers get strong and ready for writing, while we learn about different countries!

slides.text: Begin replacing pieces together, starting with the largest countries like Brazil. Point out how each country has a different shape and color.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's start with the biggest piece - that's Brazil! Look how each country has its own special shape and color, just like each country has its own special people and places.

slides.prompt: Allow your child to practice removing and replacing puzzle pieces, offering gentle guidance when needed. Discuss the shapes, colors, and relative sizes of different countries.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now it's your turn to be the map builder! Take your time feeling each piece and figuring out where it belongs. Every country has its perfect spot, just like every piece in our puzzle.

items: Can you show me how to hold the puzzle pieces properly?
Which country piece is the biggest on our South America map?
What continent are we learning about today?
slides.mascot_explanation: You're becoming such a great geographer! Let's talk about what we discovered about South America and how all these countries fit together like puzzle pieces.

slides.text: Complete the puzzle together one final time, then carefully store all pieces. Discuss how South America is one continent made up of many different countries.
slides.mastery_check: Child can hold puzzle pieces with proper pincer grip and place at least 3-4 pieces correctly with minimal assistance
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful map work you did today! Now you know that South America is a big continent with many different countries, each with its own special shape and place in the world.

title: Read-Aloud: Chrysanthemum's First Day School Adventure
description: Reading Kevin Henkes' 'Chrysanthemum' to explore themes of self-acceptance, dealing with teasing, and celebrating what makes each person unique. This story resonates with children starting school and learning about differences.
instructions: 1. Gather children in comfortable read-aloud area with good lighting
2. Show the book cover and ask children to predict what the story might be about
3. Introduce the author Kevin Henkes and mention other books by him they might know
4. Read the story with expression, showing pictures to all children
5. Pause during reading to discuss Chrysanthemum's feelings about her name
6. Ask children about times they felt different or special about their own names
7. Discuss how the music teacher helped Chrysanthemum feel better about herself
8. Connect the story to classroom values of accepting and celebrating differences
materials_needed: Paper and crayons for name writing activity
Chart paper for group discussion notes
parent_notes: This book addresses important social-emotional themes that are particularly relevant for young children navigating peer relationships and developing self-identity. Use this as an opportunity to discuss family values about treating others with kindness and appreciating differences. The story also provides a natural opening to talk about your child's own name - its meaning, origin, or why it was chosen. Connect the book's message to real-life situations your child might encounter, helping them develop empathy and self-advocacy skills.

slides.heading: Read-Aloud: Chrysanthemum's First Day School Adventure
slides.subheading: Discovering how our names make us special and unique
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi there, story lover! I'm Betsy Bear and I'm so excited to share a wonderful book with you today. It's about a little mouse named Chrysanthemum who learns something very important about her special name!

items: Chrysanthemum by Kevin Henkes
Comfortable seating area or mat
Paper and crayons for name writing activity
Chart paper for group discussion notes
slides.setup_instructions: Create a cozy reading space where everyone can see the book clearly. Have materials ready for the follow-up name activity.
slides.mascot_explanation: Let's get comfortable in our special reading spot! We have everything we need for a wonderful story time, plus some fun activities to do after we read about Chrysanthemum.

slides.text: Show the book cover and invite predictions about the story. Discuss the illustration and what children notice about the main character.
slides.mascot_explanation: Look at this beautiful book cover! What do you think this story might be about? I can see a little mouse - I wonder what adventures she'll have with her special name.

slides.text: Begin reading the story with expression, pausing to show illustrations and allow children to absorb the emotional content of Chrysanthemum's experiences.
slides.mascot_explanation: As we read, pay attention to how Chrysanthemum feels about her name. Sometimes she feels happy, and sometimes she feels sad - just like all of us do about different things!

slides.prompt: Read the complete story, stopping to discuss key moments when Chrysanthemum feels good or bad about her name. Talk about the music teacher's role in helping her feel better.
slides.mascot_explanation: Listen carefully to how the other characters treat Chrysanthemum and how that makes her feel. Think about how we can be kind to others about the things that make them special and different.

items: How did Chrysanthemum feel about her name at the beginning versus the end?
What did the music teacher do to help Chrysanthemum feel better?
